children.value: This is how the network recalls a complete pattern from noisy or partial input.
is a type of recurrent neural network where all neurons are connected to each other. It is commonly used for

: storing and recalling patterns. The network evolves to a stable state, recalling a stored pattern when given noisy or incomplete input. We can understand its behavior using matrix and vector operations.

:
Each neuron has a binary state, either
(inactive). The state of all neurons is represented by a vector

children.html: s\mathbf{s}s

children.value: . For a network of 3 neurons, the state vector might be:

children.html: s=(1−11)\mathbf{s} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}s=⎝⎛​1−11​⎠⎞​

children.value:
:
Neurons are connected by weights, represented by a symmetric matrix

children.html: WWW

children.value: . For a 3-neuron network, the weight matrix might look like:

children.html: W=(0w12w13w210w23w31w320)W = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & w_{12} & w_{13} \\ w_{21} & 0 & w_{23} \\ w_{31} & w_{32} & 0 \end{pmatrix}W=⎝⎛​0w21​w31​​w12​0w32​​w13​w23​0​⎠⎞​

children.value: The diagonal elements are zero because a neuron does not connect to itself.

:
To update the state of neuron

children.html: iii

children.value: , we calculate the weighted sum of inputs from all other neurons:

children.html: hi=∑jwijsj=(Ws)ih_i = \sum_{j} w_{ij} s_j = (W \mathbf{s})_ihi​=j∑​wij​sj​=(Ws)i​
hi>0h_i > 0hi​>0
si=1s_i = 1si​=1
hi<0h_i < 0hi​<0
si=−1s_i = -1si​=−1

children.value: Let us consider a 3-neuron Hopfield network with the following weight matrix:

children.html: W=(01−1101−110)W = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}W=⎝⎛​01−1​101​−110​⎠⎞​

children.value: Assume the initial state of the network is:

children.html: s=(1−11)\mathbf{s} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}s=⎝⎛​1−11​⎠⎞​

children.value: We update each neuron based on the weighted inputs:

children.html: h1=W11s1+W12s2+W13s3=0⋅1+1⋅(−1)+(−1)⋅1=−2h_1 = W_{11}s_1 + W_{12}s_2 + W_{13}s_3 = 0 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot (-1) + (-1) \cdot 1 = -2h1​=W11​s1​+W12​s2​+W13​s3​=0⋅1+1⋅(−1)+(−1)⋅1=−2
h1<0h_1 < 0h1​<0

children.value: , the new state of neuron 1 is

children.html: s1=−1s_1 = -1s1​=−1
h2=W21s1+W22s2+W23s3=1⋅1+0⋅(−1)+1⋅1=2h_2 = W_{21}s_1 + W_{22}s_2 + W_{23}s_3 = 1 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot (-1) + 1 \cdot 1 = 2h2​=W21​s1​+W22​s2​+W23​s3​=1⋅1+0⋅(−1)+1⋅1=2
h2>0h_2 > 0h2​>0

children.value: , the new state of neuron 2 is

children.html: s2=1s_2 = 1s2​=1
h3=W31s1+W32s2+W33s3=(−1)⋅1+1⋅(−1)+0⋅1=−2h_3 = W_{31}s_1 + W_{32}s_2 + W_{33}s_3 = (-1) \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot (-1) + 0 \cdot 1 = -2h3​=W31​s1​+W32​s2​+W33​s3​=(−1)⋅1+1⋅(−1)+0⋅1=−2
h3<0h_3 < 0h3​<0

children.value: , the new state of neuron 3 is

children.html: s3=−1s_3 = -1s3​=−1

children.value: After one round of updates, the new state of the network is:

children.html: s=(−11−1)\mathbf{s} = \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}s=⎝⎛​−11−1​⎠⎞​

children.value: A Hopfield network stores specific patterns by adjusting the weights, and it evolves to minimize its
. The energy of the network is defined as:

children.html: E=−12∑i,jwijsisj=−12sTWsE = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} w_{ij} s_i s_j = -\frac{1}{2} \mathbf{s}^T W \mathbf{s}E=−21​i,j∑​wij​si​sj​=−21​sTWs

children.value: As the network updates, it reduces its energy until it reaches a stable state, which corresponds to one of the stored patterns.
are represented by a state vector

children.html: s\mathbf{s}s

children.value: between neurons are represented by a matrix

children.html: WWW

children.value: Neurons update their states based on the weighted sum of inputs, which is a matrix-vector multiplication.
The network evolves to stable patterns by minimizing its energy function.
This is a basic introduction to the Hopfield network using matrix and vector operations.
